speaker: interviewer
text: --Adler On November 21, 1995 in Tamarac, Florida. Now, where did you live when you worked in the hospital in Shanghai? In the hospital. And when you left the hospital after three years, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I remember correctly, I rented a room in somebody's apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what did you do to support yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I enlisted in the International Police, Shanghai International Police, in which I was up to the time the Japanese came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: From when to when about?

speaker: interviewee
text: While I was working in the hospital, I already worked also in the police. It was two shifts. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] What did you say? Where I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where you lived.

speaker: interviewee
text: I rented a room with some-- by somebody. And I guess I have three, three years with the police department over there. I made good money. I was able to open a thrift store, or a second-hand store.

speaker: interviewer
text: A thrift store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, which went very, very good. And I was able, over the years, to add two more stores to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Jewish community in Shanghai ever help you in any way, the preexisting Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Help me in what way, financially?

speaker: interviewer
text: Economically?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, and I didn't them to. I didn't need their help.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had three stores.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the end, I had four.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what type of stores? Thrift?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two were-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Hello? Can you call back? OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you take the phone off the hook?

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the stores? Two were thrift stores. One was a made-to-measure men's store. And the last one was a store for Persian rugs, crystal chandeliers, silver flatware-- mostly, mostly European items. You imported them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I bought them from, from mostly other people who wanted to get rid of or needed money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the authorities in China encourage this? Did they ever stand in your way?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Chinese authorities were very, very helpful to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what year, approximately, was the ghetto set up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe, if I remember correctly, end of '41 or beginning of '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why was it set up? By whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Japanese.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the Japanese troops in Shanghai?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the Japanese came in, what was the government like in China? Who was running the government?

speaker: interviewee
text: Up-- pardon me. Up to that point, it was, of course, Generalissimo Chiang Kai-shek and his parlia-- his government.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Nationalist.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nationalist.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the Communists beginning to become active?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they were, because the people didn't have enough to eat anymore. And Chiang Kai-shek didn't want to listen, to give them rice. So Mao Tse-tung secured enough rice, probably through Russia, and got very popular.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what happened when the Japanese came in? Did they attack? Or did they come in peacefully?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It's, it's never peaceful with an occupation force. Of course, they arrested the ones which were known to work against them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There were kidnappings. There were shootings. And they had the sanction of the German Consulate and the German government, so they knew exactly what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were being told what to do--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --By the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, to a certain extent.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your personal life like then? Did you have Chinese friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I had plenty of Chinese friends. I made an extremely good living with my stores, being that I spoke the language fluently, which helped, of course. And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] what should I tell you?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was known. I was very well known in the Chinese community. Yes, I got married to a Chinese actress. I had a son with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rose Lowe.

speaker: interviewer
text: L-O-W--

speaker: interviewee
text: L-O-W-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you marry Rose?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe it was in '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your son's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Peter.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: '42, I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to Rose and Peter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both of them disappeared one afternoon. As the story went, they were kidnapped. And I never heard anything about it, even though I searched high and low through Red Cross and any institution I could. And  to this very day, nobody knows.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your son, too, was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, why was she kidnapped, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was an active Nationalist, a famous actress working for, for the Chiang Kai-shek government.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who do you think kidnapped her?

speaker: interviewee
text: As the rumor went, Chinese Communists.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why would they take the little boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Your, your thoughts are as good as mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And! Was this published in the newspapers that she was kidnapped?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who ran the newspapers there at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chinese Communists.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they published that she was kidnapped?

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as I remember, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Meanwhile, what was happening with the ghetto establishment by the Japanese? When was that set up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, immediately when they got in. I don't think it took them more than a week's time. It was very easy to set up.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that? 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was it easy?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very easy to set up because most, or almost all refugee camps, were in this area. Most of the people from the 18,000 refugees-- I would assume that 15,000 lived in those camps. And the ones who found employment or had money could live anywhere they wanted to. So most of it-- the Japanese had it easy knowing that 15,000, or whatever the number was, lives here and made the borders and said, this is the ghetto. From this point on, you can't get out.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were not living there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were living in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. Yep.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were alone at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was not a real name. It was the section of the city of Shanghai which was called Hongkew.

speaker: interviewer
text: H-O-N-G-K-E-W?

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hongkew. Did it have barbed wire around it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Open borders.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who guarded the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our own people had to, designated by the Japanese.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they police in uniforms? The Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Armbands.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to wear a Star of David?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not a star. But when on duty-- no, they had-- they had small, little emblems which you had to wear-- metal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you lived in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still running your businesses?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you allowed to go in and out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only when I applied for a passport to go buying merchandise. And this was a very complicated process, because the Japanese officer was a terrible, terrible man.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a Mr. Goya-- G-O-Y-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his job?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the deputy director of the Emperor's Office for Stateless Refugees.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened in the ghetto with you? How did you live in the ghetto? Food--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I still had my stores and made a good living, being that I had lots of Chinese and Japanese customers. So living-- as far as living was concerned, I didn't have no problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about the other people in the ghetto, did they have a problem?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those who were in camps did not have a problem, because they got food and, and lodging. I believe it was subsidized by the American Joint.

speaker: interviewer
text: Joint Distribution Committee.

speaker: interviewee
text: Joint Distribution. And those who were not in camps, we made a living. No question about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were allowed to work, the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, you were allowed to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: The nationality of the Jews in the ghetto was what? Where did they come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say overwhelmingly German. However, we had Austrians, Poles, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] some White Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they get along with each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beautifully.

speaker: interviewer
text: All the Jews got along with each other? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't say all. There was one particular group which I prefer not to disclose in the interview because it might be ill-taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they German Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly not, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And during this time, what was your relationship with the Chinese?

speaker: interviewee
text: Excellent.

speaker: interviewer
text: And with the Japanese?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very carefully-- you had to know whom to approach, when to approach, how to approach.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said this Director Goya was a terrible person.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that manifest itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, by beating-- by beating people when they applied for passports, by calling them liars, by calling them miserable Jews, by withholding passes to get out of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he personally do this, or had others?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he did it personally.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Small, short, miserable creature.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he beat them?

speaker: interviewee
text: With his hand, with his fist.

speaker: interviewer
text: During that time, did you know what was happening in Europe during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know everything. But we had, of course-- through radio, we found out. Or through newspapers, we found out. So we knew what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know there was a Holocaust going on in Europe? A Holocaust.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no, not really. We heard rumors about it. It was easier for me, being that I was part of it right at the beginning. So I could believe the things I have heard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you elected to any office in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was. I was a chief of a residential section called Pao, Pao Chia--

speaker: interviewer
text: P-A-O--

speaker: interviewee
text: P-A-O C-H-I-A. And later on, to work in the Japanese office to help people to apply for passports, and also as an observer of what's going on, more or less, with this Mr. Goya.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you start any other businesses at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, I couldn't start anything, no, because I could not get out, out of the ghetto as much as, as the rest.

speaker: interviewer
text: What finally happened in the ghetto to-- at the end of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: It just was opened up.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe it was in the summer of '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you say August?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who opened it up?

speaker: interviewee
text: By decree.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that after the Japanese were A-bombed-- atom-bombed-- would you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hiroshima and Nagasaki.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But I forgot to tell you one thing, that in 19-- between '44 and '45, the Americans bombed us every single night like clockwork at 12:37 after midnight. The sirens broke out. And the bombing started, including one Sunday afternoon [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] where 17 of our people got killed by bombs.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any theory about why the Americans were bombing? Do you think they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Japanese installations, mostly. But of course, you know, there are always some strays. And the Japanese put up installations in Hongkew. So they probably provocated the bombings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when the ghetto was opened up, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- I opened one store [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] in the French Concession, which was my rug store.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your what store was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rugs, Persian rugs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, rugs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Chandeliers, silverware, flatware, and mostly European items.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get those items?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to buy them from people-- from our people who wanted to sell their merchandise.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could we pause here briefly? I do believe that when you worked-- I'm taking you back now to Dachau, briefly. The day that you were released from Dachau, what happened that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I forgot. The day I was released-- on the day of your release, you had to stand in line waiting for the trains to come in. And I took my pajama number which I had in Dachau. I wanted to take it with me as a souvenir. And on that particular day, Mr. Eichmann came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Adolph Eichmann?

speaker: interviewee
text: Adolph Eichmann came to visit Dachau. And somebody must have told him that I carried a number with me. And sure as hell, he came. They pointed me out. He came, searched me, found the number. And I got a pretty good kicking from him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What area of the body was kicked by him?

speaker: interviewee
text: The groin area, the rear end.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did Adolf Eichmann look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Miserable, little creature. Skinny, skinny creature.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's now go back to China.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're out of the ghetto. You started a business. What happened next? Where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived-- this was a very large store. And behind the store was a little apartment. And I lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the same section of Shanghai?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, this is in the French Concession.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in the elegant part of Shanghai.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had many Chinese friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever start another business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not after that, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have a factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I'm sorry. I took-- when the-- I took a Chinese partner, who was influentional-- influential in Chinese politics and knew quite a few government people. And we opened a office machine factory by being able to see the lord mayor of Shanghai, who gave us introductions to Chiang Kai-shek, who in turn wrote letters for every government in China to buy this machine. We did extremely well. We had to expand to a 24-hour, around-the-clock factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you became a wealthy man in Shanghai.

speaker: interviewee
text: Extremely.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of that machine company?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shanghai Royal Machine Corporation.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in China?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till I got my affidavit and a passage to get out, which was in 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you want to get out? Any particular reason?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because the Communists were in China. And I didn't want to experience-- they confiscated all my money, what I had, so.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that about, '48?

speaker: interviewee
text: The, the confiscation, you mean? No. First, the Japanese confi-- confiscated in '44. And then [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I believe the Communists took the rest away in '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: And yet you still had a business running?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I wanted to do my own living.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they let you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did Chiang Kai-shek leave China? Would you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would-- I believe it was in '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you tried to get an affidavit to leave China?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because of the Communists?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you successful in getting the affidavit?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was successful that I got it in '47. And till all the papers and everything else was in order, it was '48 to leave. However, I think-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I overlooked something which I just wanted to tell you, and I lost it in my mind. I'll come back to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're remembering a lot. Now when you got the affidavit, did you have any--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Excuse me for interrupting you now. I was supposed to leave Germany in 1938 in the month of December. I had a number from the United States government, a 10,000-- a 10,000 number, which was two at that time. While I was in the concentration camp, an American vice consul, in cooperation with a rabbi, made of big business in selling low numbers. By the time I came out of the concentration camp, I had to get a new number, which would have been due leaving Germany in 1944 or 1945, which was useless. Pardon me for--

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: --overlooking it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then you were fortunate enough to get the passage of the Jewish man who passed away. You picked up his passage.

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct. Correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you used his passage.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's why I had to use it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Now we go back to the affidavit. Who signed the affidavit for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My two cousins in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: One was Bert Adler, and Manfred Adler.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what-- did you have any bureaucratic problems leaving China?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because I went through the regular procedure-- x-rays, and everything else. The consulate checked everything out and--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about leaving China?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would have never left if the Communists wouldn't have come in. I made a fantastic living and--

speaker: interviewer
text: But they took it all away.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took it away. And of course, I'm happy I'm here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you came to New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you travel from China to New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: An ocean liner. I think it was the I believe-- not quite sure. And from-- I wanted to stay in San Francisco. But my cousins insisted since they had responsibility over me, I have to come to New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I arrived in New York? Let me-- [LAUGHS] let me figure it out. 38, 37.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you settle in New York? Where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was lucky. I found a job at Montefiore Hospital. And I rented a room by people in the Bronx. And that's how it started.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you settled in the Bronx.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do in Montefiore Hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Operating room technician.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you trained for that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I-- in the meantime I had a lot of training, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you worked in the operating room for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till 1952. Three years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then--
